title: Artemis II's state-of-the-art space toilet broke
author: Gail Sherman
summary: Just hours into its historic mission to return to the moon for the first time in half a century, Integrity suffered a mission-critical failure — the toilet. Shortly after entering Earth orbit, the fan in the Universal Waste Management System (UWMS) redesigned for the Orion capsule failed to start. — Read the r
full_text:
Just hours into its historic mission to return to the moon for the first time in half a century, Integrity suffered a mission-critical failure — the toilet. Shortly after entering Earth orbit, the fan in the Universal Waste Management System (UWMS) redesigned for the Orion capsule failed to start.

NASA-assisted mission specialist Christina Koch worked to troubleshoot the jammed fan as other crew members set up a backup system. According to Space.com, the problem was determined to be a controller issue, and the toilet was reported to be fully operational within a few hours.

In an article published before the plumbing failure, Scientific American reported on the history of the space toilet, from the plastic bags used by the Apollo astronauts to the latest version of UWMS created for the Artemis program. 

Early toilets on both the space shuttle and the International Space Station (ISS) used [a] vacuum system—with the key difference being that the ISS model recycled some wastewater, whereas the space shuttle's version vented it into space. Both systems were significantly improved over the "toilets" of the Apollo years but still had big limitations. They weren't built with female anatomy in mind and couldn't process urine and feces at the same time, and while they provided some semblance of privacy with a curtain, there wasn't yet a solid door.

An early version of UWMS has been installed on the International Space Station since 2020, adding the capability of simulated liquid and solid waste collection, as well as the much-desired door for privacy. The design was updated for Artemis' lunar mission, and the scientists who worked on the project are "eager to learn whether the mission's four astronauts are happy with the design."Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen gave a tour of a training mockup of the system on YouTube.
